messages.content: Stop + capital + max positions: MA 50/200 on top 50 with an 8% stop, ₹10 lakh capital, max 10 positions.

I'll run the MA 50/200 crossover with your specified risk controls.Here are the real results (2019–2026, ~7.5 yrs):

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Trades | 86 |
| Win % | 38.4% |
| Total return | +240.4% |
| CAGR | 17.8% |
| Max drawdown | −17.6% (−₹1.76L) |
| Net P&L | +₹24.0L |

**How the risk controls changed things** (vs. your earlier unconstrained 50/200 run):

- **Drawdown dropped hard:** −43.8% → **−17.6%**. The 8% stop and the 10-position cap are doing exactly what you'd want — capping how much any single loser or crowded book can bleed.
- **Trades fell** 204 → 86. The max-10-positions cap means when a golden cross fires but the book is full, that signal is skipped. So you're trading a subset of opportunities.
- **Return held up well:** 281% → 240%. You gave up ~40 pts of raw return but slashed the worst-case pain by more than half — a clearly better risk/reward trade.
- **Win % is low (38%)** — normal for trend-following. The system makes money because winners run far bigger than the many small stops (classic low-hit-rate, high-payoff profile).

**Honest caveats:**
- 86 trades over 7.5 years is a **thin sample** — results lean on a handful of big trends. Don't over-trust the precise CAGR.
- The 8% stop on a *slow* 50/200 system can chop you out during normal pullbacks, then re-enter later — some of that lost 40 pts is exactly that.
- Return divides by full ₹10L capital, but with max 10 positions you may often be **under-deployed** (cash drag), so the "true" return on capital-at-work is arguably higher.

Want me to sweep the **stop (e.g. 8/12/15/none)** or **max_positions (5/10/15)** to see the trade-off cleanly? A quick grid would show whether 8% is too tight.
Trailing stop: MA 50/200 on top 50 with a 12% trailing stop.

I'll run 50/200 on top 50 with a 12% trailing stop (keeping ₹10L / 10 positions consistent with your last run so it's comparable).Results with a 12% trailing stop (₹10L, max 10 positions):

| Metric | 12% Trailing | 8% Fixed (prev) |
|---|---|---|
| Trades | 138 | 86 |
| Win % | 47.8% | 38.4% |
| Total return | **+109.9%** | +240.4% |
| CAGR | **10.4%** | 17.8% |
| Max drawdown | −14.2% (−₹1.42L) | −17.6% (−₹1.76L) |
| Net P&L | +₹11.0L | +₹24.0L |

**The read — the trailing stop hurt returns here:**

- **Return roughly halved** (240% → 110%). A trailing stop locks in gains but also **cuts winners short** during normal pullbacks. In a low-hit-rate trend system, the *whole edge* is letting a few big winners run for years — the trail keeps ejecting you before the trend fully plays out. More trades (138 vs 86) confirms you're getting knocked out and re-entering repeatedly.
- **Drawdown improved only modestly** (−₹1.76L → −₹1.42L). So you paid ~₹13L of profit to shave ~₹34k off the worst drawdown — a **poor trade-off** on this system.
- Win % rose (more small trailing exits land green), but that's cosmetic — the payoff-per-winner shrank.

**Bottom line:** for a slow 50/200 trend-follower, a tight 12% trail fights the strategy's own logic. The MA death-cross *is already the exit* — layering a trail on top mostly whipsaws you. Your **8% fixed-stop version was clearly the better all-rounder** (better return AND acceptable drawdown).

If you still want a trail, it likely needs to be **much wider** (20–30%) so it only catches genuine trend breaks, not routine dips. Want me to sweep `trail_stop_pct` at **[15, 20, 25, none]** to find where it stops hurting?
Max hold: RSI reversion on top 40 with a 10-day max hold.

I'll run RSI reversion on top 40 with a 10-day max hold. I'll keep the RSI settings from your earlier run (period 14, oversold 30, exit 55) so it's comparable.Results with a 10-day max hold (RSI 14, oversold 30, exit 55, top 40):

| Metric | 10-day Max Hold | No Cap (prev) |
|---|---|---|
| Trades | 464 | 399 |
| Win % | 60.3% | 71.9% |
| Total return | **+40.7%** | +237.3% |
| CAGR | **4.7%** | 17.8% |
| Max drawdown | −32.0% (−₹3.20L) | −46.6% (−₹4.66L) |
| Net P&L | +₹4.07L | +₹23.7L |

**The read — the 10-day cap gutted this strategy:**

- **Return collapsed** (237% → 41%). The max hold force-exits trades that hadn't yet recovered to RSI 55. In mean-reversion, beaten-down names often take **weeks**, not days, to bounce — you're cutting them at the worst point, before the reversion completes.
- **Win % dropped** 72% → 60%. The extra trades (464 vs 399) are exactly these premature exits, many at a loss, that would otherwise have recovered.
- **Drawdown did improve** in rupees (−₹4.66L → −₹3.20L) — the cap stops a losing trade from bleeding indefinitely. But you paid ~₹19L of profit for ~₹1.5L less drawdown. **Terrible trade-off.**

**Note on the earlier run:** be careful — that no-cap version had a nasty −46.6% drawdown. That's the real weakness here. RSI mean-reversion "catches falling knives," and without *some* stop a losing position can keep sinking. A 10-day time-stop is the wrong tool (too blunt); a **price stop-loss** is the right one.

**Better direction:** instead of a time cap, add a `stop_loss_pct` (e.g. 8–12%) to cut the genuine losers while letting winners take the weeks they need. Want me to sweep `stop_loss_pct` at **[8, 12, 15]** (no max-hold) to tame that −47% drawdown without killing the return?
